question_text: The SPIN selling framework, developed by Neil Rackham, structures consultative selling questions in which sequence?

options.b: Solution, Plan, Implementation, Negotiation.
options.c: Situation, Problem, Implication, Need-payoff.
options.d: Specific, Practical, Insightful, Negotiable.

question_text: Geoffrey Moore's value proposition formula begins with the words:

options.b: "For [target customer] who [stated need], our offering..."
options.c: "Our mission is to [purpose] for [target audience]..."
options.d: "We help companies that [their challenge] by [our offering]..."

question_text: In a classic marketing funnel (AIDA), the stages are:

options.b: Acquisition, Interaction, Decision, Adoption.
options.c: Awareness, Interest, Desire, Action.
options.d: Audience, Insight, Direction, Advocacy.

question_text: 'Value-based pricing' differs from 'cost-plus pricing' in that:

options.a: It sets price by perceived value to the customer.
options.b: It is always more expensive than cost-plus pricing.
options.c: It adds a higher profit margin on top of costs.
options.d: It is used only in services and not in products.

question_text: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV or LTV) is most accurately defined as:

options.a: The total cost of acquiring one new paying customer.
options.b: The number of years a customer remains active with your firm.
options.c: Total revenue expected from a customer over their lifetime.
options.d: The size of the first purchase made by a paying customer.

question_text: Which of the following is generally TRUE of B2C selling vs B2B selling?

options.a: B2B has no emotional component in the buyer's decisions.
options.b: B2C requires no relationship-building with the customers.
options.c: B2C cycles are shorter; B2B involves more stakeholders.
options.d: B2B always has higher transaction value per deal than B2C.

question_text: In sales, a 'smokescreen' objection is best described as:

options.a: An objection sent in writing rather than raised verbally.
options.b: An objection raised by a non-decision-maker in the room.
options.c: A price objection raised in the final stage of a deal.
options.d: A stated objection that hides the real underlying concern.

question_text: The classic 4 Ps of marketing are:

options.b: People, Profit, Process, Promotion.
options.c: Positioning, Pricing, Persuasion, Process.
options.d: Plan, Produce, Promote, Profit.

question_text: In negotiation theory, ZOPA stands for:

options.b: Zone of Possible Agreement.
options.c: Zero-Point Agreement Authority.
options.d: Zone of Permitted Activity.

question_text: Cialdini's six principles of influence include all EXCEPT:

options.b: Reciprocity.
options.c: Aggression.
options.d: Authority.

question_text: The 80/20 rule (Pareto principle) in account management typically suggests:

options.a: About 80% of deals should close in 20% of the time available.
options.b: About 20% of time on existing customers and 80% on new ones.
options.c: About 80% of revenue comes from about 20% of customers.
options.d: About 80% margins are preserved by capping discounts at 20%.

question_text: If a sales team has a 25% conversion rate from qualified leads to closed deals, and needs ₹50 lakhs in closed business, with an average deal of ₹5 lakhs, how many qualified leads do they need?

options.b: 10 leads.
options.c: 20 leads.
options.d: 40 leads.

question_text: In marketing, 'brand marketing' and 'demand marketing' differ primarily in that:

options.a: Brand and demand are the same activity described in two different ways.
options.b: Brand builds long-term preference; demand drives short-term sales.
options.c: Brand is used only in B2C contexts; demand is used only in B2B ones.
options.d: Brand is digital while demand is offline in most modern marketing.

question_text: When meeting a potential client for the first time, what percentage of the conversation should ideally be you talking vs them?

options.a: Roughly 50/50 — balanced conversations are most effective.
options.b: Roughly 70% you, 30% them — to share what you offer.
options.c: Roughly 30% you, 70% them — to understand their context.
options.d: It depends entirely on the client's stated preference.

question_text: You are an MBA intern. Your manager assigns you to meet a prospective client. The MOST effective opening approach is:

options.a: Ask directly what their budget for the project is to qualify the opportunity early.
options.b: Present your company's capabilities thoroughly to establish strong credibility upfront.
options.c: Open with friendly conversation about non-work topics to build personal rapport first.
options.d: Open with broad questions about their priorities and what would make this meeting useful.

question_text: Three meetings into a complex sale, you realise the person you have been engaging cannot actually approve the purchase. The MOST appropriate next move is:

options.a: Bypass the current contact and approach the actual decision-maker at the company directly.
options.b: Continue with the current contact since they have been engaged in the conversation so far.
options.c: Ask: "Beyond yourself, who else would typically be involved in a decision like this one?"
options.d: Send a formal proposal anyway — the decision-maker will see it eventually through them.

question_text: A prospective client says: 'Your price is significantly higher than competitors.' The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Defend the price firmly: "We're worth it because of the quality we consistently deliver."
options.b: Agree and acknowledge the pricing gap, then move directly to closing the conversation.
options.c: Ask: "What's the comparison — same scope, support, outcomes?" Then quantify the value.
options.d: Offer a 15 percent discount to bridge the gap between the two prices being compared.

question_text: In a SPIN-selling conversation, a client says: 'Our current system is slow.' Your strongest follow-up is:

options.a: "What impact does the slowness have on productivity, customer experience, or revenue?"
options.b: "Our system is the fastest one currently available in the market for your use case."
options.c: "How much faster would you like the current system to be for your team's needs?"
options.d: "Many of our clients report similar issues with the current systems they use."

question_text: You have a strong idea for a new product feature but need engineering's buy-in to build it. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Meet the lead engineer: share customer evidence, ask reaction, explore feasibility together.
options.b: Build a working proof of concept yourself first before raising it with the engineering team.
options.c: Send a written proposal to engineering and formally request that they implement it soon.
options.d: Go to senior leadership first to get top-down support before engaging engineering team.

question_text: You just lost a major deal you were sure you would win. The MOST useful next action is:

options.a: Move on quickly to the next opportunity — dwelling on losses is generally unproductive.
options.b: Lower your prices for similar future deals to become more competitive in the market.
options.c: Identify a more junior buyer in the same company to start a fresh sales conversation.
options.d: Schedule a brief debrief: ask honestly why they chose differently and listen openly.

question_text: You need to send a cold email to a senior executive at a target company. The MOST effective opening is:

options.a: A short, specific message: reference their work, share one insight, ask for 15 minutes.
options.b: A flattering paragraph about their company and their notable accomplishments to date.
options.c: A comprehensive overview of your company's capabilities and your full range of offerings.
options.d: A direct ask for a meeting without any introduction or context provided beforehand.

question_text: Your client has stated they will 'think about it' for the third time in three meetings. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Apply pressure with an artificial deadline or scarcity message to force a decision now.
options.b: Walk away from the deal entirely and reset the relationship for a later opportunity.
options.c: Ask: "What are you still working through — fit, timing, price, or something else?"
options.d: Send a follow-up email reiterating the value proposition for the client's consideration.

question_text: In a B2B sale, the procurement contact is pushing for a 30% discount but the technical buyer loves your product. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Concede the full discount to keep procurement happy and protect the overall deal timing.
options.b: Escalate to the technical buyer's senior leadership to override procurement's request.
options.c: Refuse the discount firmly to maintain pricing integrity across all other customers.
options.d: Engage both: validate procurement's role, use technical buyer enthusiasm to expand scope.

question_text: A long-time client is happy with your service. The MOST effective approach to growing the account is:

options.a: Offer aggressively discounted bundle deals on additional services to tempt expansion.
options.b: Run a business review on evolving challenges, then propose solutions tied to priorities.
options.c: Aggressively pitch additional services in every meeting to surface every opportunity.
options.d: Wait for them to ask for more services when they have a new need on their side.

question_text: You need to inform a client that a project will be delayed by 6 weeks. The MOST effective communication is:

options.a: Wait until the original deadline approaches to inform them, hoping the situation improves.
options.b: Have your senior manager deliver the news instead to preserve your client relationship.
options.c: Communicate the news only by email so the conversation is fully documented in writing.
options.d: Communicate now: explain cause, action being taken, revised plan, meaningful accommodation.

question_text: You are assigned a new strategic account. The FIRST action you should take is:

options.a: Send a polished introduction email outlining your role and how you can help their team.
options.b: Schedule introduction meetings with as many people in the account as possible quickly.
options.c: Send your best case studies and customer references to demonstrate value upfront.
options.d: Research the account: business model, recent news, priorities, structure, prior history.

question_text: A client signed up for one of your services 6 months ago and is satisfied. They are not aware of your other offerings. The MOST appropriate cross-selling approach is:

options.a: Wait for them to discover your other offerings on their own through organic channels.
options.b: Offer them a discount on a second service to encourage them to try it out soon.
options.c: In the next review, ask about priorities and mention one relevant additional service.
options.d: Send them a comprehensive catalogue of all your services to surface every possible offering.

question_text: After a first meeting, a prospect says: 'Just send me a proposal.' The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Ask: "Happy to — can we take 15 more minutes so the proposal reflects what matters most?"
options.b: Decline to send anything until the prospect commits to scheduling a follow-up meeting first.
options.c: Send a generic capabilities document instead of a specific proposal at this early stage.
options.d: Send a comprehensive formal proposal within 24 hours to capitalise on the early momentum.

question_text: In a complex B2B sale, you have one enthusiastic internal advocate at the prospect company. The MOST effective use of this relationship is:

options.a: Maintain only formal communications with them to avoid any appearance of favouritism.
options.b: Rely on the advocate to sell internally on your behalf to the broader decision group.
options.c: Equip them: understand internal politics, prepare materials, brief on objections, attend key.
options.d: Bypass the advocate and engage the senior decision-makers directly to move things faster.

question_text: You are representing your company at a trade show. A visitor approaches your booth and looks interested but says little. The MOST effective opening is:

options.a: Launch directly into a polished elevator pitch about your product to capture their interest.
options.b: Ask: "What brought you to the show today? Are you exploring something specific in mind?"
options.c: Offer a free giveaway item to engage them and build a moment of positive interaction.
options.d: Stay silent and wait until they speak first to indicate what they are actually looking for.

question_text: A potential client wants to buy directly from you, but your company has a channel partner who covers that region. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Refuse to engage and tell the client to find the right partner on their own time.
options.b: Connect them: "Our partner handles this region — I'll introduce you and stay involved."
options.c: Sell directly to the client — closing the deal is ultimately what matters most for revenue.
options.d: Sell to them directly and quietly offer the channel partner a referral fee privately.

question_text: A client's annual contract is coming up for renewal in 60 days. The MOST effective preparation is:

options.a: Run a structured business review: revisit goals, document value, address concerns, discuss.
options.b: Wait for the client to initiate the renewal conversation when they are ready to discuss.
options.c: Offer an aggressive renewal discount upfront to lock them in early before any competition.
options.d: Send a standard renewal email 30 days before contract expiry to maintain the relationship.

question_text: When meeting someone with a problem, my instinct is to ask questions before recommending solutions.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am confident discussing money, pricing, and commercial terms with clients or stakeholders.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can step back from operational details and think about the bigger strategic picture.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable negotiating, even when the other party is more senior or experienced.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can usually understand what is most important to a client even when they do not say it directly.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: After a 'no', I can come back with a thoughtful follow-up rather than giving up.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable doing quick mental math (percentages, margins, conversions) in business conversations.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can use stories and examples effectively to illustrate business points.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am comfortable asking directly for what I want (a decision, a referral, an introduction).

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I find selling and persuasion uncomfortable, even when I believe in what I'm selling.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I find it difficult to ask for a price or fee that reflects the true value of what I provide.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: How often do you rehearse important business conversations or pitches in advance?

options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you read books, case studies, or analyses related to business, strategy, or sales? PILLAR 02  ·  LEADERSHIP & INFLUENCE Self-leadership, situational leadership, influence without authority, decision-making under uncertainty, and the foundational leadership behaviours that distinguish strong managers from average ones. Items in this pillar: 50

options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: Hersey and Blanchard's Situational Leadership model proposes that effective leadership style should vary based on:

options.a: The personal leadership style preferences of the leader involved.
options.b: The industry context in which the team is currently working.
options.c: The development level of the team member for the specific task.
options.d: The size of the organisation in which the team operates.

question_text: Servant leadership, as articulated by Robert Greenleaf, places the primary responsibility of the leader as:

options.a: Serving the growth and success of those they lead.
options.b: Modelling perfect behaviour for followers to copy.
options.c: Achieving organisational results through firm control.
options.d: Making the important decisions on behalf of the team.

question_text: Daniel Goleman's four domains of emotional intelligence are:

options.b: Thinking, feeling, acting, and reflecting on outcomes ahead.
options.c: Listening, speaking, reading, and writing across all contexts.
options.d: Awareness, empathy, action, and influence over self and others.

question_text: Kotter's 8-step change model begins with which step?

options.b: Form a guiding coalition.
options.c: Develop a vision and strategy.
options.d: Create a sense of urgency.

question_text: The OODA loop — used in military and business decision-making — stands for:

options.b: Outcome, Objective, Decision, Action.
options.c: Order, Operate, Deliver, Audit.
options.d: Observe, Orient, Decide, Act.

question_text: 'Confirmation bias' in decision-making is the tendency to:

options.a: Confirm critical details twice before taking any action.
options.b: Confirm decisions through input from multiple stakeholders.
options.c: Follow the opinion of the most senior decision-maker.
options.d: Favour information that confirms one's existing beliefs.

question_text: Bruce Tuckman's model of team development stages are:

options.b: Starting, Building, Maturing, Concluding.
options.c: Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing.
options.d: Setup, Sprint, Synthesise, Solidify.

question_text: According to Daniel Pink's research (Drive, 2009), the three core elements of intrinsic motivation in modern work are:

options.b: Autonomy, Mastery, Purpose.
options.c: Salary, Title, Recognition.
options.d: Safety, Belonging, Esteem.

question_text: Michael Porter's Five Forces framework analyses competitive intensity in an industry through:

options.a: Customer, competitor, capability, channel, and cost.
options.b: Marketing, sales, operations, finance, and HR functions.
options.c: Team capabilities, technology, brand, capital, culture.
options.d: New entrants, suppliers, buyers, substitutes, rivalry.

question_text: In delegation, telling someone exactly what to do and how to do it is most appropriate when:

options.a: The task is creative and open-ended in its outcome.
options.b: You want to maximise their learning and personal growth.
options.c: They are new to the task and have low competence in it.
options.d: They have high competence in the task and need direction.

question_text: You have just been appointed team leader of a 6-person team you joined recently. On Day 1 in the role, the MOST effective first action is:

options.a: Spend 2 weeks in 1:1 listening: their work, frustrations, aspirations, what they need.
options.b: Continue everything exactly as the previous leader did to provide continuity for the team.
options.c: Make a visible quick-win decision early to establish your authority and credibility.
options.d: Announce your vision and immediate priorities to the team to set clear direction quickly.

question_text: A team member you inherited is consistently missing deadlines. Their previous manager rated them well. The MOST appropriate first step is:

options.a: Issue a formal warning to set very clear expectations about the standard required.
options.b: Have a private conversation: share observations, ask what's happening, explore what's needed.
options.c: Ignore the issue — they were rated well before, so the problem might resolve itself soon.
options.d: Assume the person is coasting and quietly reassign their work to other team members.

question_text: Your senior manager asks you to deliver a project in 4 weeks. You believe it requires 8 weeks for quality delivery. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Refuse the 4-week timeline outright as unachievable within the existing constraints.
options.b: Agree to the 4 weeks to show commitment, then renegotiate the timeline later as needed.
options.c: Accept the deadline and plan to deliver a lower-quality version within the 4 weeks.
options.d: Go back: "For 4 weeks I'd need to cut X from scope or get Y resource. Which works?"

question_text: Your manager (who is senior to you) made a comment in a recent meeting that you believe was inappropriate. You feel uncomfortable but unsure whether to raise it. The MOST professionally mature response is:

options.a: Submit a formal complaint to HR to escalate the issue through official company channels.
options.b: Stay silent — they are senior to you; it is not your place to raise it with them.
options.c: Request a private chat: "When you said X, I felt Y. I wanted to share because I respect us."
options.d: Discuss your discomfort with peers first to validate whether you are overreacting to it.

question_text: You need engineering's cooperation on a marketing initiative, but they have no incentive to prioritise it. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Escalate the request to senior leadership to mandate cooperation from the engineering team.
options.b: Build your case in writing and rely on email exchanges to make the request to them.
options.c: Meet the engineering lead: understand priorities, frame work as serving their goals too.
options.d: Offer to do all of the work yourself if the engineering team supplies guidance only.

question_text: In a leadership meeting, the senior VP makes a strategic claim you believe is factually wrong. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Add gently: "Building on that — one data point that might add nuance to the picture."
options.b: Counter directly with: "Actually that's not quite right — let me explain why it isn't."
options.c: Stay silent — openly disagreeing in this kind of public forum can be career-limiting.
options.d: Send the VP a private email after the meeting ends with the correct information attached.

question_text: A junior team member proposes an approach that you think is sub-optimal but not seriously flawed. The MOST developmentally effective response is:

options.a: Tell them the right way to do it from your experience to save the team valuable time.
options.b: Let them try it, ask questions to surface risks, let them adjust as they learn from doing.
options.c: Veto their approach and reassign the work to a more experienced team member instead.
options.d: Approve their approach without any further comment to encourage their initiative going forward.

question_text: You notice a high-performing team member is showing signs of burnout (working late constantly, withdrawal, missed details). The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Force them to take a vacation by mandating they take time off from work immediately.
options.b: Quietly redistribute some of their current work to other team members without telling them.
options.c: Wait for them to come to you when they are ready to ask for help with their workload.
options.d: Have a caring private chat: share observations, ask how they are, work on workload together.

question_text: In a leadership team of 8, you notice the two female members rarely speak in strategic discussions. The MOST appropriate action as a peer is:

options.a: Ask them privately what is working and isn't, then advocate for changes to meeting dynamics.
options.b: Publicly call on them by name during meetings to actively encourage their participation.
options.c: Raise the lack of voice diversity to leadership as a structural issue needing attention.
options.d: Conclude they are introverted by nature and let them speak when they feel comfortable doing so.

question_text: Your team can pursue Project A (large, slow, transformational) or Project B (small, fast, quick win). Resources allow only one. The MOST strategically rigorous approach is:

options.a: Defer the decision entirely to your manager since they hold the final accountability.
options.b: Pick A — bigger payoff usually means bigger career impact over the long term for the team.
options.c: Pick B — quick wins build credibility and momentum for the team to take on bigger work.
options.d: Evaluate each on expected value and risk; choose with explicit reasoning shared with team.

question_text: Your team has just delivered a project that failed publicly. The MOST appropriate first action with the team is:

options.a: Identify the people who are most responsible for the failure and address them privately.
options.b: Wait for senior leadership's reaction before responding so you can align the messaging.
options.c: Move on quickly to the next project to maintain team morale and forward momentum.
options.d: Run a retrospective: focus on systemic causes; own the failure publicly as the leader.

question_text: Your manager asks you to slightly misrepresent project status to a senior leader to avoid difficult conversations. The MOST professional response is:

options.a: Push back: "I'm not comfortable misrepresenting. Can we discuss what's honest?"
options.b: Comply with the request since they are your manager and they have called the judgement here.
options.c: Report the manager to their senior leader to escalate the ethical concern through channels.
options.d: Comply with the request but document your discomfort about the situation in writing.

question_text: A direct report keeps asking you to solve problems for them. The MOST developmentally effective response is:

options.a: Refuse to solve any of their problems and tell them they need to figure them out alone.
options.b: Move them to a role with less complexity so they can deliver more reliably for the team.
options.c: Solve the problems efficiently for them to save the team's time across the whole quarter.
options.d: Ask coaching questions: "What have you tried? What are the options? Which would you choose?"

question_text: Your team has had an exceptional quarter. The MOST effective way to recognise them is:

options.a: Request a modest team bonus from leadership to acknowledge the team's hard work fairly.
options.b: Wait until the annual review to formally recognise the team in performance documents.
options.c: Combine specific public recognition by name with meaningful non-monetary gestures together.
options.d: Send out a generic "great job team!" message at the end of the quarter to thank them.

question_text: Your CEO asks you to take on a high-visibility initiative that would seriously over-extend you. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Accept enthusiastically — career opportunities at this level of visibility are quite rare.
options.b: Refuse the request to protect your current commitments and the quality of existing work.
options.c: Show enthusiasm, share current capacity honestly, offer alternatives or partial scope.
options.d: Accept the request and quietly deprioritise existing work to make room for the new ask.

question_text: A project you led has just been completed successfully. You're invited to present results to senior leadership. The MOST effective leadership posture is:

options.a: Send team members to present in your place so they get direct exposure to senior leaders.
options.b: Present the project as the team's success, naming individual contributions where they shaped it.
options.c: Insist that the entire team present together to share the spotlight as a group effort.
options.d: Present it as your own personal achievement to maximise your career visibility with leaders.

question_text: You are interviewing for an open role on your team. One candidate has strong skills but a few values you find concerning. Another candidate has weaker skills but very strong values fit. Time-to-hire is pressure. The MOST appropriate choice usually is:

options.a: Hire the values-fit candidate if core capability allows them to grow into the role over time.
options.b: Hire the strong-skills candidate quickly to relieve the immediate pressure on the team.
options.c: Continue the search for a perfect candidate who has both strong skills and values fit.
options.d: Hire the strong-skills candidate — skills tend to be harder to teach than values overall.

question_text: Two of your direct reports are in open conflict that is affecting the team. The MOST effective intervention is:

options.a: Tell them to resolve the conflict between themselves without further manager involvement.
options.b: Meet each separately, then facilitate jointly: shared goals, agreements, accountability.
options.c: Restructure the team so the two of them don't have to interact regularly going forward.
options.d: Take a side with whichever of them is correct on the substantive matter under dispute.

question_text: You receive 360 feedback indicating you are perceived as 'too aggressive' and 'difficult to disagree with'. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Identify specifically who said the feedback and address their concerns directly with them.
options.b: Apologise publicly to the entire team for any past behaviour that may have hurt people.
options.c: Reflect: is there truth? Pick 1-2 behaviour changes, share publicly, ask team to call out.
options.d: Dismiss the feedback — strong leaders sometimes need to be assertive to drive things forward.

question_text: Your team has been doing things the same way for years. The MOST effective way to introduce innovation is:

options.a: Announce mandatory new processes that the team must adopt to break the old habits decisively.
options.b: Wait for organic change to happen — forcing innovation tends to breed strong resistance.
options.c: Hire external consultants to redesign the team's workflows from a fresh objective perspective.
options.d: Run small time-bounded experiments: try a new approach for 4 weeks, measure, decide together.

question_text: You have been asked to reduce your team by 20%. The MOST humane and professional approach is:

options.a: Decide on capability needs, deliver news personally, provide support, communicate openly.
options.b: Apply pure performance criteria to make objective decisions about who to keep on the team.
options.c: Announce the reductions broadly to the company first, then handle individuals afterwards.
options.d: Delegate the difficult conversations entirely to HR to handle on behalf of the company.

question_text: You want to drive a major change across the organisation. You have one senior sponsor but limited authority. The MOST effective coalition-building approach is:

options.a: Build the change quietly behind the scenes and announce it publicly when it is ready.
options.b: Move quickly to demonstrate visible momentum before opposition has time to mobilise against it.
options.c: Rely on your sponsor to use their formal authority to drive the change across the org.
options.d: Map stakeholders, meet each individually, listen first, share vision, build coalition before push.

question_text: A major decision you championed has just failed publicly. The MOST effective leadership posture in the days that follow is:

options.a: Defend the decision firmly until the evidence against it becomes truly overwhelming for all.
options.b: Distance yourself from the decision quickly to protect your reputation with senior leadership.
options.c: Take some leave from the role to reset emotionally before returning to lead the team.
options.d: Own the decision publicly, share what you've learned, show you are still steady and engaged.

question_text: You are leading a virtual team across 4 countries. Meetings are dominated by 2 high-context cultures while the others stay quiet. The MOST effective intervention is:

options.a: Call on the quieter members specifically by name during meetings to draw them into discussion.
options.b: Continue with the meetings as they are now — those who want to participate eventually will.
options.c: Redesign meetings: pre-shared agendas, round-robin input, written async channels for thinkers.
options.d: Split the team into separate regional teams to reduce cross-cultural communication friction.

question_text: I lead myself effectively without needing external supervision or pressure.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can make good decisions even when I don't have complete information.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can articulate a clear picture of what I'm trying to achieve, and inspire others around it.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: People who work with me would say I am trustworthy and consistent.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I am willing to have difficult conversations rather than letting issues fester.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)

question_text: The Pyramid Principle (Barbara Minto) for structured business communication recommends:

options.a: Present arguments in chronological order of their discovery.
options.b: Lead with the main message, then support with grouped arguments.
options.c: Start with background context and build up to the conclusion.
options.d: Present every possible counter-argument upfront before yours.

question_text: The 'Big Five' personality traits (OCEAN) include all EXCEPT:

options.b: Extraversion.
options.c: Neuroticism.
options.d: Aggressiveness.

question_text: The book Difficult Conversations (Stone, Patton, Heen) identifies three layers in every difficult conversation:

options.a: Past, present, and future of the issue.
options.b: Facts, opinions, and underlying emotions.
options.c: What happened, feelings, and identity.
options.d: Listener, speaker, and topic in the discussion.

question_text: Nancy Duarte's research on great presentations highlights which structural pattern:

options.a: A linear progression of setup, conflict, and resolution stages.
options.b: Alternating between 'what is' and 'what could be' for contrast.
options.c: An academic structure of hypothesis, methodology, findings.
options.d: A strict three-act structure borrowed from classical theatre.

question_text: Amy Cuddy's research on body language proposes that:

options.a: Open expansive posture can shift how confident one feels.
options.b: Body language matters mainly in face-to-face meetings.
options.c: Posture affects external perception but not internal state.
options.d: Body language reveals true intentions that cannot be hidden.

question_text: Otto Scharmer's four levels of listening progress from:

options.b: Surface → middle → deep → transcendent listening levels.
options.c: Downloading → factual → empathic → generative listening.
options.d: Passive → active → reflective → directive listening stages.

question_text: 'Getting to Yes' (Fisher & Ury) proposes the core principle of principled negotiation as:

options.a: Apply pressure until the more aggressive party prevails in talks.
options.b: Always negotiate in a setting that gives you a clear advantage.
options.c: Open with the most extreme position that you can plausibly defend.
options.d: Separate people from problem; focus on interests not positions.

question_text: Which is the MOST effective subject line for an email requesting a senior leader's decision by Friday?

options.b: "Quick question on next quarter's marketing plan."
options.c: "Important: please read this at your earliest."
options.d: "Following up on our earlier email discussion."

question_text: You have 5 minutes to present quarterly results to the CEO. The MOST effective structure is:

options.a: Walk through chronologically what happened across the team during the quarter as it unfolded.
options.b: Start with detailed methodology, then the findings, then the implications for the quarter ahead.
options.c: Lead with the headline takeaway in one sentence, then 3 supporting facts, then implications.
options.d: Present all the charts in detail and let the CEO ask questions about what matters to them.

question_text: In a meeting with a client, your senior colleague makes a commitment to the client that you believe will be hard to deliver. The MOST appropriate immediate response is:

options.a: Publicly correct your colleague in the meeting to protect the client from a future failure.
options.b: Mention the concern publicly but framed as a "just to add nuance" addition to their comment.
options.c: Stay silent in the meeting; raise the concern with your colleague privately right after it.
options.d: Subtly signal disagreement through your body language during the meeting to flag your concern.

question_text: You are negotiating with a counterpart from a culture where direct refusal is rare. They say 'we will consider it' to your proposal. The MOST appropriate interpretation is:

options.a: Insist on a clear yes or no immediately so the negotiation can move forward decisively.
options.b: Take the statement at face value — they will consider it carefully and respond when ready.
options.c: Probe gently: "Is there anything about the proposal that doesn't work that we should discuss?"
options.d: Assume it is essentially a soft yes and start planning execution of the agreement now.

question_text: A peer has been talking over others in team meetings. You want to address it without damaging the relationship. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Address the behaviour publicly in the next meeting to model the kind of behaviour you want.
options.b: Mention the issue to your shared manager and let them handle the conversation with the peer.
options.c: Privately: "I've noticed people get cut off. Wanted you to know how it's landing. Thoughts?"
options.d: Mention it indirectly with: "maybe we should all watch our talking time in meetings going forward."

question_text: You need to send a difficult email to a major client. The MOST effective approach to writing it is:

options.a: Send the first draft immediately to ensure timeliness while the situation is still fresh.
options.b: Have a long phone call with the client instead of writing because tone travels better.
options.c: Ask three colleagues to review the draft before sending it to make sure it reads well.
options.d: Draft it, re-read after an hour or overnight: check tone, clarity, sceptical-reader test.

question_text: You are about to give an important presentation and feel extremely nervous. The MOST effective in-the-moment response is:

options.a: Skip the opening pleasantries entirely to get the speaking portion over with as quickly as you can.
options.b: Apologise to the audience for being nervous upfront to set expectations about your delivery.
options.c: Reframe nerves as excitement — physiologically the body's response overlaps with excitement.
options.d: Tell yourself firmly to calm down and try to slow your breathing before going on stage.

question_text: You are presenting to a room where one senior person is openly skeptical and asks pointed questions throughout. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Match their energy and push back firmly to defend your position from their challenges.
options.b: Defer their questions to the end so that you can complete your presentation without interruption.
options.c: Engage seriously: "That's a fair concern — let me address it"; acknowledge, stay confident.
options.d: Ask the meeting chair to redirect them so the rest of the room can hear the full content.

question_text: Which of the following slide design choices is MOST effective for a business presentation?

options.a: Just the company logo and a section title to keep the slide visually clean and uncluttered.
options.b: Many supporting charts on each slide to demonstrate the analytical rigour of the work done.
options.c: Comprehensive bullet points covering all the points the presenter wants to make on a slide.
options.d: One clear headline message in a sentence, supported by one visual or 3 simple bullet points.

question_text: You need to brief your manager on a complex project decision. The MOST effective email format is:

options.a: Lead with TL;DR or "Bottom Line" first, then Background, then Considerations, then Recommendation.
options.b: Send just the recommendation and a request to schedule a meeting to discuss the details further.
options.c: Send a comprehensive narrative covering everything that happened chronologically across the project.
options.d: Send a bullet list of every consideration you analysed in detail to demonstrate thoroughness.

question_text: A long-standing client has become unreasonably demanding and frequently raises their voice in meetings. The MOST professional response is:

options.a: Escalate the situation to your manager to handle them on behalf of the account team.
options.b: Have a calm private chat outside the heat: name the pattern, state commitment, set expectations.
options.c: Tolerate the behaviour — the client pays the bills and that is ultimately what matters here.
options.d: Drop them as a client entirely since the relationship has become unworkable for the team.

question_text: A peer asks you for help on something that would take 4 hours of your time on top of your existing commitments. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Engage briefly: "I have a tight week — could you share more so I can see if there's a faster way?"
options.b: Say yes to the request — being helpful generally builds strong workplace relationships over time.
options.c: Say no without any further explanation to keep the conversation simple and time-efficient.
options.d: Say yes but quietly deliver less than they expect so the cost to you is more bearable.

question_text: A colleague is technically excellent but consistently sharp and dismissive in communication. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Have a direct chat: share one specific instance, ask if there's context, propose new norms.
options.b: Avoid the colleague and minimise interactions to keep the working dynamic tolerable for both.
options.c: Stay quiet and just let them be themselves — that is simply their personal style of working.
options.d: Mirror their communication style yourself to even out the dynamic between the two of you.

question_text: You need to influence a peer in another department to support an initiative they have no incentive to care about. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Offer them something useful in return for their support as a transactional exchange of value.
options.b: Understand their world — what they're measured on — and frame your ask in their interest.
options.c: Ask them as a personal favour with an appeal to your working relationship over the years.
options.d: Mention that senior leaders are watching the initiative to create indirect pressure to support.

question_text: In a leadership meeting, you have a strong contrarian view on the strategy being discussed. You are the most junior person present. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Wait until after the meeting and raise your concern to your direct manager privately.
options.b: Speak up: "I may be missing something — want to share a different angle here."
options.c: Stay silent through the meeting since you are the most junior person in the discussion.
options.d: Withhold the view until you are more senior and can express it without career consequences.

question_text: You are building your professional online presence (LinkedIn). The MOST effective long-term content strategy is:

options.a: Post viral content about trending topics each week to maximise reach across the platform.
options.b: Avoid posting publicly and engage only through private networking with people you already know.
options.c: Post inspirational quotes and motivational content regularly to attract a wider audience base.
options.d: Share your specific perspective on your domain over time — observations and real lessons learned.

question_text: You want to deepen your relationship with a more senior person you respect. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Ask for one specific piece of advice, follow through, return to share outcome, give back later.
options.b: Engage with their content publicly to show interest in their work and stay visible to them.
options.c: Ask them explicitly to be your formal mentor and commit to a regular cadence of meetings.
options.d: Send periodic "just checking in" messages to keep yourself on their radar over the months.

PILLAR 04  ·  MENTAL WELLNESS & RESILIENCE Career anxiety, impostor phenomenon, healthy ambition, recovery, and the sustainable performance habits required for a long management career. Items in this pillar: 35

options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)

question_text: Research on professional resilience identifies which factor as MOST important for long-term resilience?

options.a: Social support, meaning, and recovery habits — all developable.
options.b: High intelligence combined with strong educational achievements.
options.c: Avoidance of stressful situations across personal and work life.
options.d: Innate temperament that a person is born with and cannot change.

question_text: Impostor phenomenon, identified by Pauline Clance and Suzanne Imes, is best described as:

options.a: A cognitive bias only experienced by women in professional contexts.
options.b: A character flaw causing chronic self-promotion in the workplace.
options.c: A diagnosable anxiety disorder requiring formal clinical treatment.
options.d: Competent people feeling like frauds despite their achievements.

question_text: Research on sleep and professional performance has consistently shown:

options.a: Sleep needs vary dramatically between healthy individuals of similar ages.
options.b: Most adults can adapt to five hours of sleep without measurable impact.
options.c: Sleep quality matters far more than total sleep quantity for performance.
options.d: Chronic short sleep degrades cognition even when self-perception adapts.

question_text: Research by Alia Crum and others on 'stress mindset' has shown:

options.a: Stress can be eliminated entirely through regular meditation.
options.b: How we frame stress changes its physiological effect on us.
options.c: Stress is purely physiological and is unrelated to mindset.
options.d: All stress is harmful and should be minimised wherever possible.

question_text: You are 3 months into a rigorous MBA program and feeling persistently overwhelmed. The MOST constructive response is:

options.a: Push harder through the overwhelm — that level of intensity is just part of business school.
options.b: Take a temporary break from the program to reset before returning fresh to the work.
options.c: Diagnose honestly (workload, anxiety, sleep) and commit to 1-2 interventions for 4 weeks.
options.d: Drop one of your courses to lighten the load and reduce the academic burden a bit.

question_text: You feel anxious about your career trajectory while peers seem confidently ahead. The MOST healthy response is:

options.a: Work harder to try to catch up with your peers and close the perceived career trajectory gap.
options.b: Switch your career direction entirely to something less competitive to reduce the pressure.
options.c: Ask your peers directly for their honest career advice on what is working for them right now.
options.d: Recognise comparison is unreliable — refocus on your own goals, reduce comparison inputs.

question_text: You bombed a major case competition in front of your peers. The MOST psychologically healthy response is:

options.a: Take a break from all challenging activities for a few weeks to reset and recover fully.
options.b: Avoid all future case competitions to prevent the chance of a repeat public embarrassment.
options.c: Tell yourself the judges were simply wrong and your team's analysis was actually correct.
options.d: Feel embarrassed briefly, then review specific factors honestly, apply learning to the next.

question_text: You have just landed a prestigious role and you feel everyone will soon discover you are not as capable as they thought. The MOST healthy response is:

options.a: Decline the role altogether to avoid the inevitable risk of public exposure of your gaps.
options.b: Suppress the feelings completely and act with full confidence in every interaction at work.
options.c: Work double-time to ensure no one notices any gap between your actual and perceived ability.
options.d: Recognise this as impostor phenomenon — talk to a mentor, focus on the work, trust growth.

question_text: Your MBA cohort has an intense social culture with events most evenings. You are exhausted but feel pressure to attend. The MOST healthy response is:

options.a: Attend everything but leave early from each to balance social showing with rest needed.
options.b: Push through and attend everything since networking in business school really does matter.
options.c: Choose deliberately: a few high-quality events, deeper relationships, protected sleep time.
options.d: Skip all of the social events to focus exclusively on academic work and your wellbeing.

question_text: After a particularly intense week, the MOST effective form of recovery is typically:

options.a: Catch up on backlog work during the weekend to feel a sense of productive accomplishment.
options.b: Sleep in for 12 hours on Saturday morning to make up for all the missed sleep that week.
options.c: Active recovery: adequate sleep, physical activity, restorative people, low pressure.
options.d: Binge-watch TV shows over the weekend to fully switch off from anything work-related.

question_text: Over the past 3 weeks, you have been feeling persistently low, losing interest in things, and struggling to concentrate. The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Take more weekend vacations to feel better and reset before any deeper intervention is tried.
options.b: Try harder to power through it with willpower until the symptoms eventually subside on their own.
options.c: Talk to a trusted person and proactively reach out to a counsellor or wellness service.
options.d: Wait another month or so — these things often pass on their own with a bit of time.

question_text: You are working 80-hour weeks consistently. You feel productive but notice your physical and mental health declining. The MOST mature response is:

options.a: Recognise 80-hour weeks reduce effective output over time; restructure to invest in recovery.
options.b: Continue working at that intensity — short-term sacrifice for the long-term career payoff later.
options.c: Try to be more efficient within the 80 hours so the same workload feels less draining.
options.d: Take a single long vacation later in the year to compensate for the period of overwork.

question_text: In Excel, which function would you use to look up a value from one table in another table?

options.c: AVERAGE across two ranges.
options.d: CONCATENATE on key columns.

question_text: To compare proportions of a single whole (e.g., market share by company), the MOST appropriate chart type is generally:

options.b: Line chart with multiple series.
options.c: Scatter plot with grouped points.
options.d: Pie chart or stacked bar chart.

question_text: When an LLM like ChatGPT confidently produces a piece of information that is actually wrong, this is called:

options.b: Training bias.
options.c: Network lag.
options.d: Software bug.

question_text: Your company emails you asking you to urgently click a link and reset your password. The MOST appropriate response is:

options.a: Forward the email to your team to ask them for confirmation.
options.b: Verify through a separate channel before any action is taken.
options.c: Click the link immediately to comply with the urgent request.
options.d: Reply to the email asking the sender for further verification.

question_text: The European GDPR regulation broadly governs:

options.a: Personal data processing for EU residents, by any organisation.
options.b: Cybersecurity standards adopted by EU government bodies only.
options.c: Trade tariffs and customs procedures between EU member states.
options.d: Marketing standards applicable inside European Union markets.

question_text: SaaS, PaaS, and IaaS are levels of cloud services. From most managed-for-you to least, the order is:

options.b: All three are equally managed.
options.c: IaaS, then PaaS, then SaaS.
options.d: PaaS, then SaaS, then IaaS.

question_text: A/B testing in business contexts refers to:

options.a: A method for grading individual employee performance fairly.
options.b: A qualitative user research interview method for product fit.
options.c: Randomly assigning users to variants and measuring outcomes.
options.d: A two-stage software testing protocol with code review steps.

question_text: In digital marketing, 'CAC' refers to:

options.b: Click-and-Capture rate.
options.c: Conversion Audit Cost.
options.d: Customer Annual Charge.

question_text: The 'bounce rate' on a website is most accurately defined as:

options.a: Number of times a page is shared on social media by visitors.
options.b: Percentage who leave without taking any action on the site.
options.c: Percentage of pages that fail to load when visitors request them.
options.d: Percentage of visitors who return again to the site later.

question_text: When using AI tools (like ChatGPT) for business analysis, the MOST important practice is:

options.a: Verify claims and numbers from independent sources before use.
options.b: Always trust the output since models are trained on vast data.
options.c: Avoid disclosing AI use in any professional output that you produce.
options.d: Use AI only for creative tasks and never for any analytical work.

question_text: 'Agile' methodology in project management is best characterised by:

options.a: Single-person project execution to maximise speed of delivery.
options.b: Speed-prioritised delivery without any defined process steps.
options.c: Iterative delivery in short cycles with continuous learning.
options.d: Detailed long-term planning with strict milestone-gated steps.

question_text: In a financial model, what does NPV (Net Present Value) account for that simple cash-flow sums do not?

options.b: Inflation impact on currency over the period in scope.
options.c: Tax effects applied to projected revenues and expenses.
options.d: Time value of money via discounting future cash flows.

question_text: Your CEO asks for your recommendation on a major decision. You have some data but it is incomplete. The MOST effective response is:

options.a: Present the data you have, state limits, share your recommendation, note what would change it.
options.b: Recommend the safest option available to minimise the risk of being wrong on the call.
options.c: Recommend based on your intuition since the available data is incomplete for the decision.
options.d: Refuse to make a recommendation until more complete data has been gathered for analysis.

question_text: You are preparing a competitive analysis. You use ChatGPT for a first draft. The MOST appropriate next step is:

options.a: Submit the AI's output as your own work to save time and meet the deadline efficiently.
options.b: Use AI only for formatting the document and write all of the content from scratch yourself.
options.c: Make small edits to the AI's output and then submit the resulting document as your work.
options.d: Verify every claim, replace generic content with original insight, disclose AI use clearly.

question_text: You discover that your team has been storing customer email addresses in a shared spreadsheet without proper protection. The MOST appropriate action is:

options.a: Delete the spreadsheet immediately to remove the privacy risk to the customers in the data.
options.b: Quietly secure the spreadsheet's access controls without raising the issue with the wider team.
options.c: Raise it with your manager and IT, propose a secure system, review against policy and law.
options.d: Continue as before — the existing practice has worked without any issues for the team so far.

question_text: You need to present complex analytics findings to a non-technical executive. The MOST effective approach is:

options.a: Send the raw data file across in advance and offer to discuss it with them at any time.
options.b: Lead with the headline insight in plain English; 2-3 visuals; methodology in the appendix.
options.c: Present every chart from the analysis and let the executive choose what matters most to them.
options.d: Walk through the methodology in detail to demonstrate the analytical rigour of your work.

question_text: Your team is debating whether to adopt a new productivity tool that promises efficiency gains. The MOST effective evaluation approach is:

options.a: Pilot with a small group for a defined period; measure specific outcomes; decide on real evidence.
options.b: Stick with current tools — the cost of switching is generally higher than the efficiency gains.
options.c: Adopt it immediately to capture the early-mover advantage in productivity gains for the team.
options.d: Wait for industry peers to validate the tool first before the team takes the time to evaluate.

question_text: You need to convince a non-technical executive to invest in upgrading legacy technology. The MOST effective framing is:

options.a: Bring in a vendor to make the technical case directly with the executive on your behalf.
options.b: Explain the technical limitations in detail to demonstrate the urgent need for the upgrade now.
options.c: Emphasise that competitors are advancing on their stack to create a sense of urgency to act.
options.d: Translate into business: quantify cost, connect to priorities, present a phased ROI projection.

question_text: I am comfortable working with data — spreadsheets, dashboards, and analytics.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

question_text: I can translate technical concepts into business language for non-technical audiences.

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree
options.d: Agree

options.b: Disagree
options.c: Neither agree nor disagree

options.c: Sometimes (around 50% of the time)
options.d: Often (around 75% of the time)

question_text: How often do you invest time in deepening your skills with existing tools (Excel, BI tools, presentation tools, AI tools) rather than always learning new ones?

options.b: Rarely (around 25% of the time)
